difficulty_factors: Wool is a protein fiber — sensitive to alkaline pH, heat, and mechanical agitation
Oil penetrates wool's scaly cuticle and becomes trapped between overlapping scales
Dry cleaning preferred — wool felts in water with agitation

diy.title: Cooking Oil on Wool
mixSteps: Mix 2-3 drops of Dawn in 1 cup of cool water
Prepare a vinegar rinse: 1 tbsp white vinegar per cup cool water

steps.instruction: Blot any wet oil gently with a clean white cloth. Do not rub — wool felts with friction.
steps.why: Wool's overlapping scales interlock when rubbed, causing permanent felting and shrinkage.

steps.instruction: Apply cornstarch or talcum powder generously over the stain. Wait 15-20 minutes.
steps.why: Powder adsorbs surface oil mechanically — safest approach for wool.

steps.instruction: Brush off gently. If lighter, repeat. If stain persists, move to step 4.
steps.why: Multiple powder applications minimize the amount of wet chemistry needed.

steps.instruction: Dab the dilute soap solution onto the stain. Press gently — never rub or agitate.
steps.why: The surfactant emulsifies remaining oil. Minimal agitation prevents felting.

steps.instruction: Rinse by dabbing with cool water. Follow with the vinegar rinse — wool thrives at slightly acidic pH.
steps.why: Wool's optimal pH is 5.5-7. Vinegar neutralizes any alkaline residue from the soap and keeps scales flat.

steps.instruction: Lay flat on a clean towel to dry — reshape while damp. Never wring, twist, or hang wet wool.
steps.why: Wool stretches when wet and hung. Flat drying preserves shape and prevents distortion.

diy.ifStillThere: Take to a professional dry cleaner. Tell them it's cooking oil on wool and ask for POG pre-spotting before the cleaning cycle. Hydrocarbon or GreenEarth solvent is ideal for wool.
proTips: Powder absorption is the safest first step for oil on wool.
Keep water temperature below 30°C/86°F — wool felts with heat + water.
Never use enzyme detergent with protease on wool — protease attacks wool's protein structure.
diy.safety: Test any solution on a hidden seam first. Wool dyes can bleed — if you see color on your cloth, stop and take to a professional. Never use chlorine bleach on wool — it destroys the protein fiber and causes irreversible yellowing.
diy.whyThisWorks: Cooking oil is a triglyceride that penetrates wool's scaly cuticle structure. Powder absorbs surface oil. Dilute surfactant emulsifies what's trapped between the scales. The acid rinse keeps wool's cuticle scales flat and smooth, preventing that rough or matted texture.

home.title: Cooking Oil on Wool — Careful Removal
home.confidenceNote: Fresh oil stains respond well to powder + gentle surfactant. Aged oil stains on wool often need professional dry cleaning.

primaryProduct.name: Carbona Stain Devils #5 (Fat & Cooking Oil)
primaryProduct.why: Solvent-based formula safer for wool than heavy water treatment
primaryProduct.amazonSearchQuery: Carbona Stain Devils fat cooking oil

alternativeProducts.name: Dawn Ultra Dish Soap (Blue)
alternativeProducts.why: Mild surfactant emulsifies oil — use diluted (2-3 drops per cup cool water) on wool

alternativeProducts.name: Eucalan Delicate Wash
alternativeProducts.why: Wool-safe no-rinse formula with lanolin — cleans and conditions simultaneously

home.prepStep: Blot excess oil gently — do NOT rub. Apply cornstarch and let absorb 15-20 minutes before any wet treatment.

method.instruction: Blot excess oil. Apply cornstarch generously and wait 15-20 minutes. Brush off.
method.why: Physical absorption removes oil with zero risk to wool.

method.instruction: Apply Carbona Stain Devils #5 per label — test on hidden area first.
method.why: Solvent dissolves oil without the water that risks felting wool.

method.instruction: Blot gently with a clean white cloth.
method.why: Lifts dissolved oil without mechanical agitation that felts wool.

method.instruction: If residue remains: dab dilute Dawn solution (2-3 drops per cup cool water). Rinse by dabbing with cool water. Follow with vinegar rinse (1 tbsp per cup).
method.why: Surfactant handles residual oil. Vinegar restores wool's natural pH and keeps cuticle scales flat.

method.instruction: Lay flat on a clean towel to dry — reshape while damp. Never use heat.
method.why: Heat sets oil AND felts wet wool. Flat drying preserves shape.

materialWarnings.warning: Never use water above 30°C/86°F — heat + water causes irreversible felting and shrinkage.
Never use chlorine bleach or protease enzyme — both destroy wool's protein structure.
Never rub, twist, or wring wool — mechanical agitation causes felting.

ifItDoesntWork.escalation: If the oil stain persists after two careful attempts, take to a professional dry cleaner. Hydrocarbon or GreenEarth solvent is ideal for wool and dissolves cooking oil effectively.

proTips: If the wool garment is labeled dry clean only, skip home treatment and go straight to a professional.
Nonionic surfactants (like Eucalan) are the safest wet-cleaning agents for wool.
home.whyThisWorks: Oil molecules are non-polar and bond to wool through hydrophobic interactions. Absorbent powder captures surface oil physically. A mild solvent or dilute surfactant emulsifies the rest. The acid rinse maintains wool's natural pH, keeping the cuticle scales smooth.
home.timeEstimate: 20-30 minutes (may need professional follow-up for aged stains)

pro.title: Cooking Oil on Wool — Professional Dry-Side Protocol
pro.stainChemistry: Classification: Oil/Grease (lipid-based triglyceride). Cooking oils are triglycerides of C16-C18 fatty acids — vegetable oils rich in unsaturated chains (oleic, linoleic acid) that oxidize over time. On wool (keratin protein fiber with overlapping cuticle scales, pH 5.5-8.0), oil penetrates through gaps in the cuticle and becomes trapped within the cortex. Wool's natural lanolin provides some oil resistance, but cooking oil overwhelms this with volume. Once oil penetrates past the cuticle, it's harder to extract than from smoother fibers. Dry-side POG pre-spot before HC or GreenEarth cycle is the standard professional approach — exactly what Dan Eisen recommends for dryside stains.

spottingProtocol.agent: A.L. Wilson NovaBone POG or Street's POG Remover
spottingProtocol.technique: Dry-side POG pre-spot — dissolve triglyceride before cleaning cycle
spottingProtocol.instruction: Place wool face-down on clean white towel on spotting board. Apply POG to the back of the oil stain — controlled application with dauber. Tamp with bone spatula using light, even pressure. Wool is more forgiving than silk to mechanical action, but still avoid heavy rubbing — especially on fine or loosely woven wool. Watch the towel for oil transfer. Move to fresh towel position and repeat. On wool, POG penetration is slightly slower than silk because the cuticle scales create resistance — give each application a few extra seconds of dwell time.

Flush the treated area with VDS to carry dissolved oil through and into the vacuum. Feather edges outward to prevent ring formation. Wool shows solvent rings less than silk, but still feather for a clean finish. Apply compressed air to dry the area — brief and controlled, don't over-extract.

spottingProtocol.agent: Machine cleaning — hydrocarbon or GreenEarth preferred
spottingProtocol.technique: Gentle solvent cycle for complete oil removal
spottingProtocol.instruction: Run through hydrocarbon or GreenEarth cycle — both rated A (excellent) for wool. No shrinkage risk, no felting. HC solvent is particularly effective for lipid-based stains like cooking oil. Use wool-safe detergent charge. GreenEarth is the luxury option — preserves softness perfectly. PERC is rated C for wool — risk of felting if residual moisture is present during drying. If using PERC, monitor extraction carefully.

Inspect in good light. Oil residue on wool often appears as a slightly darkened area. Touch the treated area — it should feel identical to surrounding fabric, not tacky or stiff. If oil halo persists, repeat POG pre-spot and re-clean. Do not press with steam until oil is confirmed removed — steam heat will oxidize residual oil into permanent yellowing.

pro.escalation: If cooking oil has oxidized on wool (weeks old, yellowed): escalate to amyl acetate for polymerized oil. Lipase enzyme is possible on wool with caution — rated C, brief exposure only, as lipase may strip wool's natural lanolin oils. Professional judgment required. For heavily set oil on fine or cashmere wool, consider GreenEarth soak with extended pre-spot dwell.
pro.customerExplanation: We treated the cooking oil with a professional solvent that dissolves grease before running your wool garment through a gentle dry cleaning cycle specifically designed for protein fibers. Wool responds well to this approach — the oil is removed without affecting the fabric's texture or shape.
commonMistakes: Wet cleaning without proper technique — water + agitation felts wool. If wet cleaning is required, use wool-safe surfactant at cool temperature with minimal agitation.
Using protease enzyme — protease attacks wool's keratin protein. NEVER use protease on wool.
Using chlorine bleach — destroys wool protein and causes irreversible yellowing.
Over-drying in machine — excessive heat in the dryer yellows residual oil on wool.
pro.neutralization: Dry-side protocol — no acid neutralization needed. If wet-side work was performed, apply dilute acetic acid (1:10 GAA) to restore wool's natural pH of 5.5-7.0.
pro.fabricRisks: Wool risks: felting and shrinkage from water + heat + agitation (the trifecta to avoid). Dye bleeding from alkaline agents — test first. Fiber degradation from protease enzyme or chlorine bleach. Lanolin stripping from aggressive solvents — GreenEarth preserves lanolin best.

title: Cooking Oil on Wool
stainChemistry: Cooking oil is a triglyceride that penetrates wool through gaps in the cuticle scale structure. Dan Eisen notes that oil stains creep along the weave of fabric, forming a cross or T pattern — on wool, this happens more slowly than on smooth fibers because the cuticle scales create resistance. Oxidizing oils (vegetable-based) are the bigger concern: they polymerize with exposure to air and heat, becoming progressively harder to remove. Non-oxidizing oils (mineral oil) remain soluble and are easier. The first priority is treating before any heat exposure — dryer heat or pressing will accelerate oxidation and permanently set the stain.
whyThisWorks: POG solvent dissolves the triglyceride through nonpolar affinity — the solvent molecules break the van der Waals forces holding oil within wool's cuticle structure. Dry-side treatment avoids the water that risks felting. HC solvent in the cleaning cycle is particularly effective for lipid-based stains and rated A for wool. The entire process keeps wool in its comfort zone: no water, no heat, no alkalinity, no mechanical stress.

spottingProtocol.technique: Physical oil absorption — safest first step
spottingProtocol.equipment: Cornstarch or Fuller's earth, soft brush
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply absorbent powder generously. Let sit 15-20 minutes. Brush off gently. Repeat if stain lightens. On fresh cooking oil, two rounds of powder can handle the majority of the stain.

spottingProtocol.equipment: POG agent, spotting board, clean white towels, bone spatula
spottingProtocol.instruction: Face-down on clean towel. Apply POG to back of stain. Tamp gently. Wool's cuticle provides some resistance — give slightly longer dwell than silk. Watch for oil transfer to towel. Fresh towel position each round. Flush with VDS and feather edges.
Hydrocarbon or GreenEarth cycle with wool-safe detergent. Both rated A for wool. HC solvent is effective for lipid-based stains. Gentle extraction — avoid high-speed spin on wool.
Check for oil halo. Touch test — should feel identical to surrounding fabric. If residue persists, repeat POG and re-clean.

homeSolutions: Cornstarch or talcum powder: Apply generously, wait 15-20 minutes, brush off. Repeat 2-3 times for fresh stains. Zero risk to wool.
Carbona Stain Devils #5: Apply per label. Solvent-based, minimizes water contact. Test first.
Dilute Dawn (2-3 drops per cup cool water): Dab onto stain, don't rub. Rinse with cool water. Follow with vinegar rinse. Lay flat to dry.
Professional dry cleaning — for wool, this is often the best route. Ask for POG pre-spot before cleaning.
materialWarnings: NEVER use water above 30°C/86°F on wool — heat + water causes irreversible felting.
NEVER rub, twist, or wring wet wool — mechanical action felts the cuticle scales.
NEVER use protease enzyme on wool — wool IS protein. Protease digests the fiber.
NEVER use chlorine bleach — destroys wool protein and causes permanent yellowing.
NEVER use anionic surfactant (high-pH laundry detergent) — alkaline pH opens wool's cuticle scales causing felting. Use nonionic surfactant only.
commonMistakes: Hot water — wool felts in hot water. Always cool/lukewarm maximum.
Using regular laundry detergent — most are alkaline (pH 10+) which damages wool. Use wool-specific or neutral pH only.
Machine washing with agitation — the combination of water + heat + agitation is the trifecta that felts wool.
Putting in dryer before oil is fully removed — heat permanently sets residual oil.
scienceNote: Wool's cuticle is a series of overlapping keratin scales that open in alkaline conditions and close in acidic conditions. When scales open (alkaline wash, hot water, agitation), they interlock with adjacent fibers — felting. This is irreversible. For oil stains, the dry-side approach keeps wool in its natural state: scales closed, fiber relaxed. Dan Eisen's observation about oxidizing vs. non-oxidizing oils is critical for wool: vegetable oils (oxidizing) must be treated before heat exposure because polymerized oil on wool is essentially permanent — the cuticle traps the oxidized residue.
customerExplanation: Cooking oil on wool requires gentle handling because wool is a protein fiber that felts with heat or agitation. We used a professional degreasing solvent followed by a gentle cleaning cycle specifically designed for wool. The oil is removed and the fabric's natural softness is preserved.
escalation: If oil has oxidized (aged, yellowed): amyl acetate for stronger solvency. Extended pre-spot dwell time. For cashmere or fine merino, consider GreenEarth soak. Lipase enzyme is rated C on wool — brief exposure only if other methods fail.
callPro: Call a professional when: the oil stain is more than a few days old; the wool garment is labeled dry clean only; the item is cashmere or fine merino; you see felting, color change, or texture distortion during home treatment.
solventNote: POG pre-spot on dry side before cleaning cycle. HC solvent is particularly effective for lipid-based stains on wool. GreenEarth preferred for luxury wool — preserves softness and lanolin.
